text: I'm exactly like you Rob!  To get that to work with the Cu₂O is exactly what I'd try to do cuz it's cheap and easily obtainable.  I wonder what would happen if you used a flux instead of the matte medium. . .  The idea being that the flux could get the molten copper nodules to flow together.  The flux could also act as a binder to the substrate.  A really good flux is Handy Flux.

author: @Alkimi
text: I bought some conductive composite PLA filament for my cheap-o 3D printers over a year ago that I keep forgetting about. It just occurred to me that your disinterest in the tech may have let that go unnoticed! Are you aware? If you're amenable to working with carcinogens you could add one more product to your conductive ink product line! It's hard to find and little-known so you could grab a nice slice of the market. I haven't tried it yet but my research indicated that it works exceptionally well in terms of ease-of-use because it's a low-temp composite material with a high heat specificity, which also makes production as cost-effective as it can be. The cost of a consumer-grade filament extruder rig dropped right alongside the 3D printer cost. I'd love to get my next spool from you guys once I get around to working with it. I'm financially irresponsible so I had to go back to my day job as a programmer :(

author: @sum_rye
text: A lot of interesting suggestions in the comments, I am curious (as are many others) about adding flux to the ink mix, also I wonder if there could still be some moisture present in the ink. I know with 3d printing metals, vaporization is an issue as it blasts away the material next to it, causing gaps in the printed layers. And as others have mentioned I also wonder about using some of your graphite ink in the process, but I was thinking more along the line of multiple layers of materials, such as a layer of graphite ink with a layer of copper oxide on top of it. And there is something in the back of my mind about copper oxides and the Thermo Electric Generators you were experimenting with in earlier videos..... I feel like there is some possibilities there.....

replies.author: @captainnemo6655

author: @charlesurrea1451
text:
Definitely need to look at the concentration level of the ink. 
 The material melts and pools which means the carrier isn't moving out of the way. 
What's better colloid or suspension?
Then there's the different carriers for dispersal and burn. 
I'm thinking the substrate should be something more heat resistant like silicone.
You could easily put a silicone layer between the board and the ink.

author: @fleagle8tee
text: Hi Robert, You could engrave the pattern into your material first, then add the powder to the material so it sits in the engraved groove. Then turn off the air assist and re run the the engraving program to sinter the powder? You'd have to ensure your material is fixed to the bed to make sure it doesn't move in between processes. I have used this method before to infill engravings with powder coating powders of various colours.

author: @C-M-E
text: I've been looking at laser stuff the last few weeks for a horde of sintering applications, but one thing (aside from having stupid high max temperature requirements) is viability in having continuous fusion. The older laser printers were notoriously jerky in being able to connect lines, though fine motor control is obviously much better with the advancements in 3D printer stepper motors. I'm genuinely curious to see how this goes!

replies.author: @cymacymulacra2301
replies.text: It looks like the cracking and crazing is the result of rapid drying.  Perhaps some programming would fix this, such as a runny sacrificial layer sprayed on thin and lased to maybe 25% conversion, then another layer lased to 50%.  So if you pull up the copper from the substrate when  the thickness is where you want. you'd have a printed part with a roughened surface on the bottom and a shiny and fused surface on the top side.  Expensive equipment.  It must be programmable?
